draft.introduction: Airs, Waters, and Places is the Hippocratic treatise on environmental medicine, arguing that climate, water sources, and geography shape both individual health and national character. It is the earliest surviving work of medical geography and epidemiology.

translation.text: Whoever wishes to investigate medicine properly should consider the following: the seasons of the year, the winds, the quality of the waters, the position of the city with respect to sun and wind, and the ground. For from these things one may learn the diseases likely to prevail in each locality.

translator_notes.note: This treatise established the principle that disease must be understood in environmental context—an insight that anticipates modern epidemiology and public health. Its analysis of how geography shapes character also influenced Montesquieu’s theory of government in The Spirit of the Laws.
